notes: Nominative subject form. Used in John 1:1a and 1:1c.
Anarthrous nominative. In John 1:1c θεὸς ἦν ὁ λόγος the predicate theos is anarthrous, marking quality not identity. This is the Colwell construction.
John 1:1 en arche en ho logos. The imperfect signals continuous, durative being in the past — the Logos was already in existence at the beginning, not that he came into being then.
John 1:1 en arche. Dative of sphere or reference. The Logos existed within/at the beginning as its defining point.
John 1:1c predicate nominative. Anarthrous: marks divine nature/quality of the Logos, not mere identity equation.
pros with accusative in John 1:1b: pros ton theon. Signals directional presence-toward, intimate relational orientation, not mere proximity.
